data.title: EU fines Musk's X €120M for breaching bloc's online rules
data.description:
The European Commission said Friday it has fined Elon Musk's X €120M over the social media platform's process to obtain "blue checkmarks," lack of transparency of its ad repository, and restriction of access to public data, which are in breach of the bloc's Digital Services Act. 

According to the Commission, the easy availability of verified status on X makes it difficult for users to judge an account's authenticity and exposes them to scams.

They allege X uses design features and access barriers, such as excessive delays in processing, which undermine the purpose of ad repositories, and the platform also imposes unnecessary barriers on researchers who wish to access public data.

"Deceiving users with blue checkmarks, obscuring information on ads, and shutting out researchers have no place online in the EU. The DSA protects users," said Henna Virkkunen, a senior official at the EC.

FCC Chairman Brendan Carr took it to X in support of Musk's company and called out Europe and its efforts to target U.S. Big Tech. 

"Once again, Europe is fining a successful U.S. tech company for being a successful U.S. tech company," Carr said. "Europe is taxing Americans to subsidize a continent held back by Europe’s own suffocating regulations."

data.title: Nvidia reveals its biggest expansion to CUDA since its 2006 launch
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA) has launched CUDA 13.1 and CUDA Tile, which the Jensen Huang-led company said is the most substantial advancement to the platform since its release about 20 years ago.  

"This exciting innovation introduces a virtual instruction set for tile-based parallel programming, focusing on the ability to write algorithms at a higher level and abstract away the details of specialized hardware, such as tensor cores," said Nvidia engineers Jonathan Bentz and Tony Scudiero in a blog post.

CUDA is a parallel computing platform and programming model created by Nvidia. It helps developers increase the speed of their applications by harnessing the computing power of graphics processing units. 

The new tile-based programming option allows developers to have "fine-grained control over" how their code is executed, particularly across multiple GPU architectures.

"Tile-based programming enables you to program your algorithm by specifying chunks of data, or tiles, and then defining the computations performed on those tiles," Bentz and Scudiero noted. "You don't need to set how your algorithm is executed at an element-by-element level: the compiler and runtime will handle that for you."

CUDA Tile is available in Python. Nvidia plans to release a version compatible with C++ at some point in the future.

"It's just come out in Python, and I think that maps beautifully to the AI development framework," said Stephen Jones, Nvidia's CUDA architect. "We are intentionally releasing the CUDA Tile frontier language as an open-source project."

data.title: Dell warns PC customers of price increases amid rising memory costs: report
data.description:
• Dell Technologies (DELL) has warned PC customers that it could raise prices between significantly, amid rising costs for memory, Trendforce reported.
  • The price increases could be between 15% and 20% and could hit as soon as the middle of this month, the news outlet added, citing industry sources.
  • Dell did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.
  • Memory chips, which are widely assumed to account for as much as 15% to 18% of a PC cost, have been in strong demand for months, amid the continued artificial intelligence boom. HP Enterprise (HPE) said this week that its memory costs have risen, though management expressed confidence that it will be able to pass off the price increases to consumers.
  • Micron (MU), one of the largest memory suppliers, said this week that it will end its Crucial consumer business as part of its ongoing portfolio transformation to align its business to “secular, profitable growth vectors in memory and storage.”
  • Separately, Citi upped its price targets on Seagate (STX) and Western Digital (WDC) this week, citing “sustained” momentum in storage.

data.title: Stellantis in default of Canadian aid package for moving Jeep line to US
data.description:
The Canadian government will formally serve Stellantis (STLA) with a default notice in regards to its CAD500M ($358M) aid package as a result of the carmaker’s decision to move production of the Jeep Compass SUV to the U.S. from the Brampton, Ontario plant. 

"We’ll stand firm for the sake of our workers, our industries and our nation because defending these jobs means defending Canada’s economic backbone and the livelihoods of countless families,” Canadian Industry Minister Melanie Joly said to the House of Commons' international trade committee.

Stellantis' (STLA) move from Brampton to Belvidere is part of the carmakers' plan to invest $13B over the next four years in U.S. manufacturing facilities to mitigate the impact from U.S. trade policy.

In October, Joly threatened legal action against Stellantis (STLA) if the company continued with plans to move Jeep Compass production out of the country.  Joly considered a move to Illinois in violation of Stellantis’ agreement to “maintain its full Canadian footprint, including Brampton.”

“We were there for the company in 2009 to pull it back from the brink of bankruptcy, and now we expect you to be there for Canadians,” Joly wrote in a letter to Stellantis CEO Antonio Filosa, referencing the Canadian government’s contribution to the 2009 bailout of General Motors and Chrysler.

But with import tariffs threatening Stellantis’ (STLA) already fragile bottom-line, the company pledged a substantial investment to move production into the U.S. to help offset ~$1.74B in tariff costs.

Of the $13B, Stellantis (STLA) said it will allocate $600M to reopen the Belvidere, Illinois Assembly Plant to expand Jeep output. The plant, idled in February 2023 amid cost-savings measures, had been a major sticking point for the autoworker’s union, which secured assurances of its reopening during 2023 contract negotiations.

The potential legal dispute with Canada has done little to dent Stellantis’ (STLA) upward momentum with shares higher for a fourth consecutive day with a cumulative gain of 15%.

data.title: Stocks in the green as NFLX-WBD acquisition raises sentiment, investors assess inflation gauge
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages turned up on Friday, as traders awaited fresh inflation data in hopes of gauging the Federal Reserve's monetary policy, while news of Netflix ((NFLX)) acquiring Warner Bros. Discovery ((WBD)) dominated news.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.3% in midday trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.3%, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.3%. 

Streaming giant Netflix (NFLX) -1.9% announced it will acquire Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) +3.8%, including its film and television studios, HBO Max and HBO, for a total enterprise value of ~$82.7 billion.

“This creates basically a two-horse show. Disney (DIS), with physical assets and parks, similar to Comcast (CMCSA); and Netflix (NFLX) with tons of streaming and movie assets. Paramount (PSKY) [is] stuck now treading water. [David Ellison, CEO of Paramount Skydance] is the big loser here. Cable assets will all be shed from all. Disney needs to do the same next,” said Ross Gerber, co-founder, CEO and CIO of Gerber Kawasaki.

In economic news, core PCE inflation stalled at +0.2% MoM in September as expected. Meanwhile, December consumer sentiment was higher at 53.3 vs. 52.0 consensus and 51.0 in November, according to the initial UofM Survey.

“Income [is] up 0.4%, spending 0.3%. However, inflation adjusted spending was flat which implied a soft spending path heading into the holiday spending season,” said Joseph Brusuelas, RSM U.S. principal and chief economist.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 4 basis points to 4.14%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 4 basis points to 3.57%.

data.title: Bitcoin falls back to below $90K, set for weekly loss; crypto stocks slide
data.description:
Bitcoin (BTC-USD) slid back below the $90K level on Friday after this week's rebound fizzled, putting in on track for a weekly loss. Crypto-related equities were also under pressure.

The first-ever crypto (BTC-USD) declined 3.2% in the last 24 hours to $89.2K at 11:28 a.m. ET, leaving it 1.8% down in the past five trading sessions. BTC rebounded to as high as ~$94K mid-week from ~$84K on Dec. 1, before losing momentum in the second half of the week.

The intraday weakness was seen across the cryptoverse, with Ether (ETH-USD) sliding 2.1% to $3.06K, Aptos (APT-USD) -7.1%, Avalanche (AVAX-USD) -6.3%, Uniswap (UNI-USD) -5.7%, Near (NEAR-USD) -5.4%, Internet Computer (ICP-USD) -5%, Dogecoin (DOGE-USD) -4.5% and Cardano (ADA-USD) -4.3%.

Crypto-focused stocks: Strategy (MSTR) -3.5%, Coinbase Global (COIN) -1.8%, Gemini Space Station (GEMI) -1.3%, Bullish (BLSH) -2.3%, Bakkt Holdings (BKKT) -7.1%, MARA Holdings (MARA) -4.3%, Bitmine Immersion Technologies (BMNR) -3.1%, SharpLink Gaming (SBET) -2.8% and Riot Platforms (RIOT) -4.6%.

At the same time, though, the broader stock market edged higher as traders digested delayed, largely in-line inflation data for September, as well as the megadeal between Netflix and Warner Bros. Discovery.

data.title: KNOT Offshore Partners signals $963M contract backlog and evaluates $10 buyout offer as charter market tightens
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: KNOT Offshore Partners LP (KNOP) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Derek Lowe, CEO & CFO, opened the call by highlighting "the most material development during and since Q3 2025, which is our receipt of an unsolicited and nonbinding offer from our sponsor, KNOT, to buy the publicly owned common units for $10 per common unit." Lowe stated the offer is being evaluated by the Conflicts Committee with independent advisers and that "I will not be addressing that matter on today's call and would refer you to the press release that we issued on the 3rd of November and to KNOT's own 13D filing with the SEC on the same date."
  • Lowe reported revenues of $96.9 million, operating income of $30.6 million, net income of $15.1 million, and adjusted EBITDA of $61.6 million. Available liquidity at quarter end was $125.2 million, with $77.2 million in cash and $48 million in undrawn credit facilities. Utilization reached 99.9%, or 96.5% factoring in drydocking.
  • The company purchased the Daqing Knutsen, securing seven years of a guaranteed higher rate, and established a buyback program, acquiring nearly 385,000 common units for just over $3 million, concluding the program in October.
  • Two refinancings were completed: a $25 million revolving credit facility and a sale and leaseback for the Tove Knutsen, generating a net $32 million. Extensions were secured with Shell and Equinor, with Bodil Knutsen contracted through March 2029 plus two one-year options. A new time charter for Fortaleza Knutsen begins in Q2 2026.
  • Lowe highlighted "the shuttle tanker market has been tightening in both Brazil and the North Sea...driving shuttle tanker demand growth. We've extended our backlog as of September 30, 2025, to $963 million of fixed contracts averaging 2.6 years and rather more if all options are exercised."
  • Lowe stated, "We are continuing to repay debt at $95 million or more per year, which we think is prudent with a depreciating asset base. Our robust model has been validated by the 4 refinancings we've completed in the second half of 2025."
  • Lowe emphasized 93% of vessel time in 2026 is covered by fixed contracts, rising to 98% if all options are exercised.

Outlook

  • Lowe stated, "Based on current charter rates, we believe the charters' options are likely to be taken up given the strength of the charter market."
  • He added, "We believe that the current order book still trends towards a medium-term shortage of shuttle tankers when set against the forthcoming production."
  • The company announced Pernille Østensjø as a nominee for election as Independent Director at the upcoming annual meeting.

Financial Results

  • Revenues were reported at $96.3 million, operating income at $30.7 million, net income at $15.1 million, and adjusted EBITDA at $61.6 million. Available liquidity ended at $125.2 million, up $20 million from Q2.
  • Distribution declared at $0.026 per common unit, paid in November.
  • The average margin on floating rate debt was 2.2% over SOFR.
  • The company noted strong coverage in upcoming years: 93% of vessel time for 2026 and 69% for 2027 under fixed contracts.

Q&A

  • Charles Fratt, Alliance Global Partners, Research Division: Asked about potential rate change for Fortaleza Knutsen time charter versus the current Transpetro contract. Derek Lowe responded, "We don't comment on individual rates, I'm afraid, but I can say that we're certainly satisfied with the rate that we'll be getting."
  • Fratt inquired if the new contract is directionally a higher rate. Lowe stated, "Yes, we don't comment on particular rates. I mean you'll appreciate the timing of when this new contract has been signed by comparison with when the previous one was signed some years ago. And obviously, there will be a change in market conditions between the 2 times."
  • Fratt asked about the number of dry dockings planned for 2026. Lowe confirmed, "Yes, that's right," in reference to an active year with at least four, potentially five dry docks.
  • Fratt questioned if G&A costs would remain at $1.6 million per quarter after the Daqing acquisition. Lowe replied, "We're not expecting that to change materially."
  • Fratt clarified if the buyback program had concluded. Lowe confirmed, "That's right."
  • Fratt asked about the timeline for a potential definitive agreement regarding the buyout offer. Lowe responded, "I'm afraid all the information that's currently available is what's been announced already on the 3rd of November."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral tone, focusing on operational details, contract rates, and the buyout process, with pressing but not confrontational follow-ups.
  • Management maintained a neutral, factual tone in both prepared remarks and answers, often deflecting specifics on contract rates and the buyout process. Lowe used phrases like, "I'm afraid all the information that's currently available is what's been announced already."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained steady and focused on operational execution, while analysts shifted from questions about fleet growth and chartering to increased attention on the buyout and capital allocation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The most significant change from Q2 is the announcement of the $10 per unit buyout offer, which was not present last quarter.
  • Revenue, operating income, net income, and adjusted EBITDA all increased quarter-over-quarter, as did available liquidity.
  • Charter backlog expanded from $895 million to $963 million of fixed contracts, reflecting market momentum.
  • Analysts in Q3 focused more on the buyout offer and capital returns, compared to Q2’s focus on fleet acquisition and charter strategy.
  • Management’s tone and communication style remained pragmatic and consistent, with a continued emphasis on fleet utilization and prudent capital management.

Risks and Concerns

  • The ongoing evaluation of the buyout offer was highlighted as a major uncertainty, with management deferring to public filings and limiting discussion.
  • Exposure to drydocking in 2026 was acknowledged, with four to five scheduled dry docks.
  • Market conditions and charter renewals remain focal points, though management expressed confidence in market strength.
  • No significant increases in G&A costs are anticipated, even after fleet expansion.

Final Takeaway

During the third quarter of 2025, KNOT Offshore Partners reported strong financial and operational performance, increased contract backlog, and robust liquidity. The partnership is evaluating an unsolicited $10 per unit buyout offer from its sponsor amid a tightening shuttle tanker market, continued high utilization, and new charter extensions. Management remains focused on contract coverage, refinancing, and disciplined capital allocation while navigating ongoing industry and corporate developments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sen. Warner says Netflix buying Warner Bros. is `anti-monoply nightmare'
data.description:
Sen. Elizabeth Warner (D-MA) said that Netflix (NFLX) purchasing Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) looks like an "anti-monopoly nightmare."

"A Netflix-Warner Bros. would create one massive media giant with control of close to half of the streaming market," Warren wrote in a posting on X on Friday. "It could force you into higher prices, fewer choices over what and how you watch, and may put American workers at risk."

Warren's comments come after Netflix (NFLX) earlier said it will acquire Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), including its film and television studios, HBO Max and HBO, for $72 billion. 

"The Justice Department must enforce our nation’s anti-monopoly laws fairly and transparently — not use the Warner Bros. deal review to invite influence-peddling and bribery," Warren added in her post on X.

data.title: General Dynamics slips as U.K. Defence Secretary says may cancel £6.3B Ajax program
data.description:
General Dynamics (GD) -1.7% in Friday's trading after U.K. Defence Secretary John Healey said the government could cancel its £6.3 billion (~$8.4 billion) Ajax program, after the army stopped using the company's armored vehicles when more than 30 soldiers became ill while riding in them during an exercise in England last month.

Healey said he is prepared to "take whatever decision is required" and "deal with any consequences" regarding the Ajax vehicles, the British Army's first new armored fighting model in nearly 30 years, and that a decision on the future of the program will occur after the government and army conclude their investigations into the safety of the vehicles.

A senior General Dynamics (GD) employee has come under scrutiny after publicly suggesting that user errors and faulty leadership decisions by British Army crews were partly responsible for the ongoing problems with the Ajax program.

The comments were made during an online discussion about continued concerns with Ajax, which has faced years of setbacks over crew safety, vibration injuries, and system performance.

If the order is canceled, it is notclear what would replace the 589 vehicles included in the contract, or how it would affect the General Dynamics (GD) plant in Wales.

The U.K. Ministry of Defence said just last month that Ajax supports a supply chain of more than 230 companies and 4,100 jobs, and the U.K. had been in talks with other countries about potential export deals; billions of pounds have been spent on the program, with more than 160 vehicles already built.

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: KR, SVM, DDOG, GOOG
data.description:
Recent analyst updates include upgrades have been announced for Silvercorp Metals (SVM) and Datadog (DDOG), reflecting strong growth prospects and strategic business expansions. Conversely, downgrades for Kroger (KR) and Alphabet (GOOGL) indicate concerns around valuation and strategic priorities.

Upgrades

  • Silvercorp Metals (SVM): Upgrade from Hold to Buy by Alberto Abaterusso. The analyst cites robust production growth, improved margins, and strategic diversification into copper as key drivers for the upgrade.

    “SVM is on track to increase production and reduce production costs…With increased and more cost-effective production, the company will be better positioned to further improve its profit margin.”

  • Datadog (DDOG): Upgrade to Buy by Gary Alexander. The analyst highlights Datadog’s strong growth trajectory driven by multiproduct sales and AI deals, along with a solid Rule of 50 profile justifying further upside.

    “Datadog reported a strong Q3 beat-and-raise, which immediately prompted a 20%+ rally to ~$200: gains that were quickly reversed, even though the clear demand tailwinds that Datadog is showcasing appear to be well sustained. I view the volatile pullback as a great opportunity to buy Datadog as it looks to push higher, and I'm upgrading the stock to a "Buy" rating.”

Downgrades

  • Kroger (KR): Downgrade from Buy to Hold by Luca Socci. The analyst expresses concerns over Kroger’s decision to prioritize share buybacks amid rising leverage and compressing profit margins.

    “With guidance narrowed to the low end and multiples compressing…I am downgrading Kroger from buy to hold and suggest monitoring Kroger’s Q4 earnings to better understand how things are unfolding.”

  • Alphabet (GOOGL): Downgrade from Very Bullish to Neutral by Steven Fiorillo. Despite strong financial performance, the analyst notes that GOOGL’s valuation now surpasses its peers, making further capital allocation less compelling.

    “GOOGL is a great company, but it’s time to just hold its shares and reap the rewards of any further appreciation…the time to aggressively add to a position in GOOGL is well behind us.”
